text: This is a video demonstration of laparoscopic colonic pull-through and delayed colloanal anastomosis for colonic necrosis after robotic intersphincter resection. Our patient is a sixty-three year old man with long-standing hypertension and diabetes and a smoker for forty years. He is on polypharmacy and has moderate coronary and carotid calcifications. He also gives history of syncopal attacks and has passed pulmonary tuberculosis with preserved pulmonary functions.

He presented to us with a rectal cancer five centimeters from the anal verge that was moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma and had no distant metastasis. On the baseline MRI, he had a T-III lesion with mesorectal nodes. The mesorectal fascia was uninvolved, the sphincter complex was free and there were no lateral pelvic nodes.

He received long-course chemoradiation with brachytherapy boost and there was partial response on the MRI. He underwent a robotic intersphincter resection with a diverting ileostomy. He was discharged unwindfully on post-operative day six.

The final histology showed a YPT-N-Zero disease with a tumor regression grade of two by five. However, he was readmitted on day thirteen with fevers and a drain site abscess. The rectal examination demonstrated a complete anastomotic disruption with purulent discharge.

The CT scan showed complete anastomotic dehiscence with air in the pelvis and free air in the abdomen that probably tracked out along the drain site. He was taken up for emergency exploration and on perineal examination there was pus in the anal canal and the visible length of the colon had completely sloughed out.

During laparoscopic abdominal inspection, pus and slough are visible in the pelvis, with dense inflammatory adhesions between the bladder and colonic conduit. The surgeon carefully releases these adhesions using suction and continuous irrigation, ensuring to avoid bladder injuries by staying close to the pericolic fat and sigmoid wall.

The surgeon completes the pelvic dissection and focuses on separating the mesentery of the left colon from the retroperitoneum, utilizing the previously established retroperitoneal plane to avoid injury to retroperitoneal structures.

The surgeon connects the retroperitoneal and pelvic dissections, delivering the ischemic colon into the abdominal cavity for examination. The line of demarcation between healthy and ischemic tissue is clearly visible.

The surgeon thoroughly irrigates the pelvic cavity and identifies the need for additional splenic flexure mobilization. The small bowel is gently dissected away from the inferior mesenteric artery clips and the retroperitoneum, with continuous irrigation aiding the process.

The surgeon approaches the splenic flexure from the inframesocolic and suprapancreatic side, performing retroperitoneal dissection cranially over the pancreas. The lesser sac is entered, and the root of the transverse mesocolon is divided.

The surgeon approaches the splenic flexure from the lateral side, dividing the omental attachments to the left and transverse colon. This maneuver reopens the lesser sac from below, facilitating the complete mobilization of the splenic flexure and transverse colon.

The surgeon delivers the mobilized colon into the pelvis and out of the anal canal, ensuring proper orientation to prevent twisting of the mesentery. The small bowel is positioned over the colonic mesentery to prevent internal herniation. The pelvis is thoroughly irrigated, and the bladder is inspected for leaks following the repeat mobilization.
The colon is left prolapsing three centimeters out of the anal canal, with cardinal stitches placed at four locations to secure it temporarily due to slough and inflammation within the anal canal.

Seven days later, the surgeon performs a redo coloanal anastomosis under regional anesthesia after confirming the health of the colonic conduit and reduced inflammation. The final view shows the colonic conduit successfully anastomosed with good anal tone.

The patient is yet to undergo a reversal of the stoma. Despite the use of ICG in the primary operation, a long segment colonic necrosis was found. We hypothesize that this is likely due to delayed ischemia from suboptimal perfusion rather than absent perfusion.

The surgeon performs splenic flexure mobilization from the lateral side, dividing omental attachments to the left and transverse colon, facilitating tension-free anastomosis. Fluorescence from ICG is visually assessed to monitor perfusion.

During the repeat operation, the surgeon mobilizes the colon from the medial side over the pancreas body, performing a gastrocolic omental division to achieve greater length without entering the previous operative field. This demonstrates the feasibility of laparoscopic repeat operations despite acute inflammation.

This can avoid the surgical stress associated with a laparotomy in a patient with impaired physiological reserves and also reduce the wound morbidity of an open procedure. A delayed anastomosis in such setting is often safer than a primary colonal anastomosis and a two-stage operation such as this probably avoids few permanent stomas.
